items.q: What is the predicted effect of a binding price ceiling like rent control?
items.correct: Shortage in the market — quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the controlled price, leading to queues, black markets and reduced quality.
items.misconception: Price ceilings make goods cheaper for everyone with no downsides.
items.random_wrong: Price ceilings only affect the rich.
items.why: Students see price control as obviously beneficial. Standard analysis predicts shortages and unintended consequences — the trade-off is central to evaluation questions on intervention.

items.q: What is the difference between a shift in demand and a movement along the demand curve?
items.correct: A movement along the curve is caused by a change in price; a shift of the whole curve is caused by changes in other factors (income, tastes, substitute prices).
items.misconception: A change in price shifts the demand curve.
items.random_wrong: Demand always falls when supply increases.
items.why: This is the single most common error in introductory economics. Price changes cause movements ALONG the curve; the curve itself shifts only when non-price factors change.

items.q: What does standard theory predict about a minimum wage?
items.correct: If above equilibrium, it raises wages for those still employed but causes unemployment by reducing labour demand — though empirical evidence is mixed.
items.misconception: A minimum wage always reduces unemployment because workers have more money to spend.
items.random_wrong: A minimum wage is paid by the government.
items.why: Students reverse the standard prediction. Empirical evidence is genuinely debated, but the theoretical baseline is that above-equilibrium price floors create surplus (unemployment in labour markets).
The answer examiners want is: Shortage. “Surplus” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Minimum wage above equilibrium can cause:
items.correct: Unemployment if labour demand is elastic
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Unemployment if labour demand is elastic. “Labour shortage” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many main types of price control (ceiling and floor)?
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
